name: TWK Issey
classification_notes: TWK Issey belongs to the incise tradition, sitting between serif and sans-serif in the manner of Optima: no serifs, but carved inflections and variable stroke widths that reference stone-cut letterforms. Calligraphic brush gestures and rounded terminals further distinguish it from a pure geometric or grotesque sans.
summary: A calligraphic incise typeface from Rimasùu Studio, drawing on French vintage fashion invitations and Japanese aesthetics to blend stone-carved structure with brush-like gesture.
description: Sitting in the tradition of Optima, TWK Issey carries no serifs yet reads as though it might, with carved stroke inflections, varying weight contrast and rounded terminals that echo brush pressure. The design stems from a close study of French vintage fashion show invitations and Japanese visual culture, giving it a retro cadence that does not tip into pastiche. With 1,241 glyphs covering over 100 languages and spacing handled by Igino Marini, it is well-equipped for editorial layouts, visual identities and luxury brand work.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN describe TWK Issey as a calligraphic-incise typeface by Paris-based Rimasùu Studio, released in 2025, inspired by French vintage fashion show invitations and Japanese aesthetics. They position it as blurring the line between serif and sans-serif in the manner of incise classics such as Optima, with carved inflections and calligraphic softness giving it what they call a timeless quality rooted in classical references but modern in execution.

name: TWK Burns
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by ITC Avant Garde Gothic, Burns is built on pure geometric shapes with a high x-height and minimal tapering, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans in the ITC Avant Garde Gothic lineage, running ten weights from Hairline to an Ultra so heavy its strokes overlap and letters clog.
description: Designed by Nolan Paparelli and Alberto Malossi, Burns takes the pure circles and high x-height of the Lubalin tradition and pushes it to an extreme at the heavy end, where the Ultra weight produces overlapping strokes and dense, clogged forms suited to logotype work. The family holds ten weights and includes 14 stylistic sets with graphic alternates, giving it a range that spans fine editorial use to bold display applications. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini, with mastering by Jan Charvát.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN describe Burns as a geometric sans serif inspired by Herb Lubalin's ITC Avant Garde Gothic, characterised by a high x-height, minimal tapering and pure geometric shapes. They note the Ultra weight's overlapping strokes make it function as an instant logo generator, while the full ten-weight range from Hairline to Ultra remains consistent and versatile, supported by a complete character set and numerous graphic alternates.

name: TWK Everett
classification_notes: Paparelli positions Everett explicitly within the neogrotesque genre, distinguished by symmetrical structure offset by organic drawing and sharp apex details rather than strict Swiss rationalism.
summary: A neogrotesque sans originating from Nolan Paparelli's diploma work at ECAL, balancing symmetrical structure with organic drawing and sharp apex details across 22 styles.
description: Developed at ECAL/University of Art and Design Lausanne and initially inspired by the photography of Daniel Everett, the family spans Hairline to Super with matching italics in a coherent 22-style range. Low ascenders and descenders allow tight line spacing for space-economising typesetting, and eight stylistic sets extend its range across book design, poster work, branding and signage. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, mastered by Jan Charvát.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN describe Everett as a personal take on the neogrotesque genre, evolved from Paparelli's diploma work and inspired by American photographer Daniel Everett. They note its symmetrical structure balanced with organic drawing and a digital flavour, strong apex details creating visual tension, and low ascenders and descenders for tight line spacing. The family spans 22 styles covering Hairline to Super with italics, fully equipped with stylistic sets, ligatures and case-sensitive forms.

name: Everett Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width companion to a grotesque sans, Everett Mono uses uniform character blocks and mechanical ligatures that span two or three blocks, placing it firmly in the monospaced category with grotesque proportions.
summary: A fixed-width companion to TWK Everett spanning 22 styles, with mechanical ligatures that stretch across two or three character blocks.
description: Designed by Nolan Paparelli as an integral part of the Everett project from the start, the mono variant translates Everett's broad proportions and 20-weight range into uniform fixed-width blocks. Each glyph shares the same width across all weights, allowing styles to be mixed freely without disrupting the grid. A distinctive feature is the ligatures, which can occupy two or three blocks, lending the face a mechanical, almost typeset-machine quality.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN describe Everett Mono as the monospaced counterpart to Everett, noting that a mono version was always part of the project's ambition. The challenge was compressing and adjusting 20 weights of broad letterforms into single fixed-width blocks while maintaining legibility and the original Everett character. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini, mastering by Jan Charvát, and the family was published on 12 October 2021 in 22 styles with Latin Extended script coverage and eight stylistic sets.

name: Ghost
classification_notes: Ghost is a digital serif with a calligraphic axis, sharp triangular serifs and pronounced stroke contrast, inspired by graffiti lettering and calligraphy, placing it in the contemporary or expressive serif category rather than a classical historical model.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Nolan Paparelli, originating from graffiti sketchbooks and refined into a seven-weight editorial family with sharp triangular serifs and a pronounced 18-degree italic.
description: Drawn over more than a decade and originally inspired by Ghostface Killah's lettering style, the family spans Hairline through Black, each with a matching italic rethought at a steeper angle for added dynamism. The calligraphic axis and sharp triangular serifs give the lighter weights a striking delicacy at large sizes, while the bolder cuts hold well in body text. It suits editorial work ranging from fashion titles to novel covers.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN describe Ghost as a digital serif with a raw yet elegant touch, tracing its origins to street lettering and the influence of Ghostface Killah of the Wu-Tang Clan. The family grew from two styles at its 2022 launch to a comprehensive range of seven weights with italics in 2025, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini and font engineering by Studio Guillaume Besson.

name: TWK Lausanne
classification_notes: A Swiss grotesque with a clean, neutral character typical of the Geneva/Lausanne tradition, designed to function across an exceptionally wide weight range from 50 to 1000.
summary: A Swiss grotesque by Nizar Kazan spanning twenty weights from hairline to ultra-heavy, with a restrained character that has made it a fixture in fashion and cultural publishing.
description: Designed by Swiss designer Nizar Kazan for WELTKERN, TWK Lausanne runs an unusually fine-grained weight scale from 50 to 1000, each with a matching italic, giving designers granular control across a single family. Its neutrality and precision have attracted an array of high-profile clients spanning fashion houses such as Bottega Veneta and Loewe, institutions like the Museum of Modern Arts, and technology brands including Apple TV+. The name refers to the Swiss city of Lausanne, though the foundry cheekily note it is more visible in New York than in its namesake.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN present TWK Lausanne as a typeface by Swiss designer Nizar Kazan, offering twenty weights from 50 to 1000, each with a corresponding italic. The foundry's own specimen text includes a wry line noting the face is more visible in New York than in Lausanne itself, and the page showcases its use across fashion, culture and technology contexts including CDG, Raf Simons, Helmut Lang, Porsche and Apple TV+.

name: Lausanne
classification_notes: Lausanne is a Swiss grotesque in the tradition of neutral European sans-serifs, designed by Swiss designer Nizar Kazan with an exceptionally granular weight axis spanning 50 to 1000 in 50-unit increments.
summary: A Swiss grotesque by Nizar Kazan offering 20 weights from 50 to 1000, each with a matching italic, and coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.
description: Designed by Swiss designer Nizar Kazan and published through WELTKERN, Lausanne works in 50-unit weight increments from hairline to ultra-black, giving designers unusually fine control over typographic colour. The script coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek makes it practical for multilingual editorial and fashion work, as evidenced in the specimen copy referencing brands such as Post Archive Faction and Coperni. Its neutrality sits comfortably in both screen interfaces and print contexts.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN presents Lausanne as a typeface by Swiss designer Nizar Kazan, available in 20 weights from 50 to 1000 each with a companion italic, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.

name: Continental
classification_notes: The specimen text and sample strings suggest a contemporary grotesque sans with both upright and italic cuts across an extensive weight range, complemented by a Compact width variant that reinforces the grotesque classification.
summary: A large grotesque family from WELTKERN spanning nine weights with matching italics and a Compact width variant across all cuts.
description: Eighteen styles in the upright and italic, plus nine Compact variants, give Continental a broad palette for editorial, fashion and identity work. The weight range runs from Thin through to Heavy, with Book sitting between Regular and Medium for finer typographic control. The Compact subfamily allows tighter setting without switching families, making it practical across a range of formats and sizes.
foundry_blurb: WELTKERN offer Continental with a full weight range from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, alongside a Compact subfamily covering the same weights. Licences are available as a Starter Pack, with options for trials, students and editorial use.
